**Twitter, Inc.** was an American [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") company based in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), California, which operated and was named for [its flagship social media network of the same name](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") prior to its rebrand as [X](/wiki/X_(social_network) "X (social network)"). In addition to Twitter, the company previously operated the [Vine](/wiki/Vine_(service) "Vine (service)") short video app and [Periscope](/wiki/Periscope_(service) "Periscope (service)") livestreaming service. In April 2023, Twitter merged with X Holdings[[6]](#cite_note-6) and ceased to be an independent company, becoming a part of [X Corp.](/wiki/X_Corp. "X Corp.")[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Twitter was created by [Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey"), [Noah Glass](/wiki/Noah_Glass "Noah Glass"), [Biz Stone](/wiki/Biz_Stone "Biz Stone"), and [Evan Williams](/wiki/Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur) "Evan Williams (Internet entrepreneur)") in March 2006 and was later launched on July of the same year. By 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit), more than 100 million users tweeted 340 million tweets a day.[[8]](#cite_note-Twitterturns6-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Twitter_500-9) The company went [public](/wiki/Public_company "Public company") in November 2013. By 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit), Twitter had more than 330 million monthly active users.[[10]](#cite_note-Twitter-10)

On April 25, 2022, Twitter agreed to [a $44 billion buyout](/wiki/Acquisition_of_Twitter_by_Elon_Musk "Acquisition of Twitter by Elon Musk") by [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk"), CEO of [SpaceX](/wiki/SpaceX "SpaceX") and [Tesla](/wiki/Tesla,_Inc. "Tesla, Inc."), one of the biggest deals to turn a company private.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) On July 8, Musk terminated the deal.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Twitter's shares fell,[[14]](#cite_note-14) leading company officials to sue Musk in [Delaware's Court of Chancery](/wiki/Delaware_Court_of_Chancery "Delaware Court of Chancery") on July 12.[[15]](#cite_note-:02-15) On October 4, Musk announced his intention to purchase the company as he had agreed, for $44 billion, or $54.20 a share;[[16]](#cite_note-16) the agreement closed on October 27.

Following Musk's takeover, Twitter was criticized for an increase in [hate speech](/wiki/Hate_speech "Hate speech"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) systemic prioritization of [right-wing](/wiki/Right-wing_politics "Right-wing politics") content and the removal of the feature to report tweets for spreading [misinformation](/wiki/Misinformation "Misinformation").[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) His acquisition of the company has been characterized by large-scale policy changes, mass [layoffs](/wiki/Layoff "Layoff") and resignations, and a dramatic shift in the company's [work culture](/wiki/Organizational_culture "Organizational culture").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Twitter](/wiki/History_of_Twitter "History of Twitter")

### 2006–2007: Creation and initial reaction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2006–2007: Creation and initial reaction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Twttr_sketch-Dorsey-2006.jpg/250px-Twttr_sketch-Dorsey-2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Twttr_sketch-Dorsey-2006.jpg)

A sketch, c. 2006, by [Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey"), envisioning an [SMS](/wiki/SMS "SMS")-based [social network](/wiki/Social_network "Social network")

Twitter's origins lie in a "daylong brainstorming session" held by board members of the [podcasting](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast") company [Odeo](/wiki/Odeo "Odeo"). [Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey"), then an undergraduate student at [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"), introduced the idea of an individual using an SMS service to communicate with a small group.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-origins-22) Work on the project started in February 2006.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23) Dorsey published the first Twitter message on March 21, 2006, at 12:50 p.m. [PST](/wiki/Pacific_Standard_Time "Pacific Standard Time") ([UTC−08:00](/wiki/UTC%E2%88%9208:00 "UTC−08:00")): "just setting up my twttr".[[1]](#cite_note-Dorsey2006-1) The first Twitter prototype, developed by Dorsey and contractor Florian Weber, was used as an internal service for Odeo employees.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23) The full version was introduced publicly on July 15, 2006.[[4]](#cite_note-launch-4) In October 2006, [Biz Stone](/wiki/Biz_Stone "Biz Stone"), [Evan Williams](/wiki/Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur) "Evan Williams (Internet entrepreneur)"), Dorsey, and other members of Odeo formed Obvious Corporation and acquired Odeo, together with its assets—including Odeo.com and Twitter.com—from the investors and shareholders.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Williams fired Glass, who was silent about his part in Twitter's startup until 2011.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Twitter spun off into its own company in April 2007.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The [tipping point](/wiki/Tipping_point_(sociology) "Tipping point (sociology)") for Twitter's popularity was the 2007 [South by Southwest Interactive](/wiki/South_by_Southwest_Interactive "South by Southwest Interactive") (SXSWi) conference. During the event, [Twitter usage](/wiki/Twitter_usage "Twitter usage") increased from 20,000 tweets per day to 60,000.[[27]](#cite_note-27) "The Twitter people cleverly placed two 60-inch plasma screens in the conference hallways, exclusively streaming Twitter messages," remarked *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")*'s [Steven Levy](/wiki/Steven_Levy "Steven Levy"). "Hundreds of conference-goers kept tabs on each other via constant twitters. Panelists and speakers mentioned the service, and the bloggers in attendance touted it."[[28]](#cite_note-28) Reaction at the conference was highly positive. Blogger [Scott Beale](/wiki/Scott_Beale_(blogger) "Scott Beale (blogger)") said that Twitter was "absolutely ruling" SXSWi. Social software researcher [danah boyd](/wiki/Danah_boyd "Danah boyd") said Twitter was "owning" the conference.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### 2007–2022: Growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2007–2022: Growth")]

In April 2012, Twitter announced that it was opening an office in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), with the aim of working with automotive brands and advertising agencies.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Twitter also expanded its office in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin").[[31]](#cite_note-31) Twitter hit 100 million monthly active users in September 2011. On December 18, 2012, Twitter announced it had surpassed 200 million [monthly active users](/wiki/Monthly_active_users "Monthly active users").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The company had its [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") on the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") on November 7, 2013.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In September 2016, Twitter shares rose 20% after a report that it had received takeover approaches.[[34]](#cite_note-cnbc-salesforce-google-34) Potential buyers were [Alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.") (the parent company of [Google](/wiki/Google "Google")),[[34]](#cite_note-cnbc-salesforce-google-34) [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"),[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Salesforce.com](/wiki/Salesforce.com "Salesforce.com"),[[34]](#cite_note-cnbc-salesforce-google-34)[[38]](#cite_note-tc-verizon-38) [Verizon](/wiki/Verizon_Communications "Verizon Communications"),[[38]](#cite_note-tc-verizon-38) and [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company").[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Twitter's board of directors were open to a deal, which could have come by the end of 2016.[[34]](#cite_note-cnbc-salesforce-google-34)[[41]](#cite_note-41) However, no deal was made, with reports in October stating that all the potential buyers dropped out partly due to concerns over abuse and harassment on the service.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) In 2017, Twitter revamped its dashboard to improve the new user experience[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) and Stone came up with a purpose statement for the company: "Twitter exists to serve the public conversation".[[47]](#cite_note-47)

In April 2021, Twitter announced that it was establishing its African headquarters in [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana").[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In January 2022, Twitter finalized the sale of MoPub to [AppLovin](/wiki/AppLovin "AppLovin"). The deal was first announced in October 2021, and the selling price was reported at $1.05 billion.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### 2022 and 2023

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2022 and 2023")]

#### Acquisition by Elon Musk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Acquisition by Elon Musk")]

Main article: [Acquisition of Twitter by Elon Musk](/wiki/Acquisition_of_Twitter_by_Elon_Musk "Acquisition of Twitter by Elon Musk")

Businessman [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk") revealed that he had bought 9.1% of Twitter for $2.64 billion on April 4, 2022.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-sec-13-d-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) In response, Twitter's stock rose by as much as 27% and Twitter shares experienced the largest intraday surge since Twitter's IPO in 2013.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Musk was appointed to Twitter's board on April 9, under a deal that prohibited him from acquiring more than 14.9% of the company.[[52]](#cite_note-sec-13-d-52)[[55]](#cite_note-:9-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)

Musk made an unsolicited offer on April 14 to acquire Twitter for $44 billion and take the company private.[[57]](#cite_note-sec-13-d-2-57) Publicly, Musk expressed that his offer was motivated by concerns with how the company was managed, emphasizing his concern that policies he described as [censorship](/wiki/Internet_censorship "Internet censorship") were leading to a lack of [free speech](/wiki/Free_speech "Free speech") on the platform.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) Twitter's board introduced a "[poison pill](/wiki/Shareholder_rights_plan "Shareholder rights plan")" strategy on April 15, which would allow shareholders to buy additional stock should a [hostile takeover](/wiki/Hostile_takeover "Hostile takeover") occur as a means to block Musk's takeover.[[60]](#cite_note-60) On April 20, Musk secured $46.5 billion in funding as a [tender offer](/wiki/Tender_offer "Tender offer"), which the board accepted on April 25.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

On July 8, Musk announced he was unilaterally terminating the proposed acquisition, claiming in a regulatory filing that Twitter was in "[material breach](/wiki/Material_breach "Material breach")" of several parts of the agreement by refusing to comply with Musk's requests for spambot account data and dismissing high-ranking employees.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) In response, Twitter [board chair](/wiki/Board_chair "Board chair") [Bret Taylor](/wiki/Bret_Taylor "Bret Taylor") pledged to pursue legal action against Musk at the [Delaware Court of Chancery](/wiki/Delaware_Court_of_Chancery "Delaware Court of Chancery") with the goal of completing the acquisition.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) On July 12, Twitter opened a lawsuit against Musk to force the sale to proceed.[[68]](#cite_note-68) On September 13, 2022, Twitter shareholders voted to approve Elon Musk's takeover of the company.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

On October 4, 2022, it was reported that Musk offered to proceed with the deal at the original offer price of $54.20 per share.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71) The deal closed on October 27, 2022, with the merger between Twitter, Inc. and X Holdings II, Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of X Holdings I, Inc., wholly owned by Musk,[[72]](#cite_note-ownership_NYSE-72) and Musk took control of the company,[[73]](#cite_note-73) paying $54.20 per share, or about $44 billion.[[74]](#cite_note-closed-74)

By January 2023, Twitter only had around 2,300 employees left, according to Musk, with only 550 working full-time.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) By April, there were less than 1,500 active employees left[[79]](#cite_note-79) with 80% of Twitter's work force gone within a year.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

#### Post-acquisition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-acquisition")]

On October 28, immediately following Musk's acquisition, he fired CEO [Parag Agrawal](/wiki/Parag_Agrawal "Parag Agrawal") and CFO [Ned Segal](/wiki/Ned_Segal "Ned Segal").[[81]](#cite_note-81) Twitter's stock was suspended from trading prior to the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") opening the next day and was [delisted](/wiki/Delisted "Delisted") on November 8.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) On October 31, Musk announced in a security filing that he had dissolved the board of directors and would serve as the CEO of the company.[[84]](#cite_note-Euronews-84) In January 2023, he also fired an employee who criticized him.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

On November 4, Twitter laid off about half of its 7,500 employees,[[86]](#cite_note-86) leaving several internal departments, including communication and core engineering teams, understaffed.[[87]](#cite_note-verge-87) Some of the employees were later asked to return, with Twitter stating that they were "laid off by mistake".[[88]](#cite_note-88) Several current and former Twitter employees sued the company for violations of the [Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act of 1988](/wiki/Worker_Adjustment_and_Retraining_Notification_Act_of_1988 "Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act of 1988") due to failures to provide a 60-day notice prior to mass firings.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Top security, compliance, content moderation, and sales executives resigned due to Musk stating that employees must either work longer hours or resign from the company.[[91]](#cite_note-twoweeks-91) On November 16, Musk issued an "ultimatum" to all remaining Twitter employees warning that workers could expect "long, intense hours of work" if they decided to stay, a decision that was ordered to be made by the following day, with news sources reporting numerous employees leaving on the same day.[[92]](#cite_note-npr-ultimatum-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) *[The Verge](/wiki/The_Verge "The Verge")* reported that multiple critical engineering teams "completely or near-total[ly] resigned", increasing internal fears of a collapse of Twitter.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

Within a week of Musk's acquisition and subsequent internal instability of the firm, including layoffs of Twitter's trust and safety teams, large advertisers including [General Mills](/wiki/General_Mills "General Mills"), [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer"), [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen"), and [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") announced an advertising pause.[[96]](#cite_note-96) [Advertising agencies](/wiki/Advertising_agency "Advertising agency"), including [IPG](/wiki/The_Interpublic_Group_of_Companies "The Interpublic Group of Companies") and [Omnicom Media Group](/wiki/Omnicom_Group "Omnicom Group"), advised clients to pause ad spending on Twitter temporarily until the platform can sufficiently assure [brand safety](/wiki/Brand_safety "Brand safety") concerns.[[97]](#cite_note-97) Apprehension over increased hate speech made advertisers leave in large numbers. Impersonator accounts had increased following errors in the blue tick scheme for verified users causing concerns.[[98]](#cite_note-98) Musk said Twitter was experiencing "a massive drop in revenue" causing losses of $4 million per day, which he attributed to "activist groups pressuring advertisers," adding that "nothing is working" to lure advertisers back.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

According to a November 2022 report from [Media Matters for America](/wiki/Media_Matters_for_America "Media Matters for America"), Twitter had lost half of its top 100 advertisers, which spent $750 million on ads in 2022.[[100]](#cite_note-100) Advertising research firm Standard Media Index said advertiser spending on Twitter declined 55% in November year-on-year, and declined 71% in December, despite those holiday months typically seeing higher ad spending.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

By December 2022, Musk's [family office](/wiki/Family_office "Family office") privately sought to sell new shares valued at his original acquisition price of $54.20, amid turmoil among users and advertisers, and debt payments approaching. Musk's money manager wrote to investors that in recent weeks the family office had received "numerous inbound requests to invest in Twitter."[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103) *The Wall Street Journal* reported in January 2023 that talks had been held to raise up to $3 billion to pay down some of the $13 billion in bank debt incurred in the deal, which includes a $3 billion high interest rate [bridge loan](/wiki/Bridge_loan "Bridge loan"). Musk denied the report, saying, "corpo media shills clearly have their marching orders to write hit pieces on me these days."[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) *The Journal* reported in October 2023 that the seven major banks that provided the $13 billion loan had been unable to follow the [standard practice](/wiki/Syndicated_loan "Syndicated loan") of quickly selling down their exposure to other banks, due to a lack of appetite for the debt since Musk took over. The banks expected to mark down the debt by at least 15% in order to sell it.[[106]](#cite_note-106)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

In April 2023, Twitter, now under the ownership of Musk, designated [National Public Radio](/wiki/NPR "NPR") as "US state-affiliated media",[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108) a label "that's typically been reserved for foreign media outlets that represent the official views of the government, like Russia's [RT](/wiki/RT_(network) "RT (network)") and China's [Xinhua](/wiki/Xinhua_News_Agency "Xinhua News Agency")."[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111) This move by Twitter sparked a controversy and accusations of pro-[Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") bias.[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) In a statement to *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")*, NPR president and CEO John Lansing condemned the decision.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

Twitter, Inc. was merged with [X Corp.](/wiki/X_Corp. "X Corp.") in April 2023.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) X Corp. itself was merged with Musk's startup [xAI](/wiki/XAI_(company) "XAI (company)") in March 2025, nearly 2 years later.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

## Services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Services")]

### Twitter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Twitter")]

Main article: [X (social network)](/wiki/X_(social_network) "X (social network)")

Twitter was an American [social networking service](/wiki/Social_networking_service "Social networking service") that was rebranded as [X](/wiki/X_(social_network) "X (social network)") in 2023. Users could share concise text messages, images, and videos through "[tweets](/wiki/Tweet_(social_media) "Tweet (social media)")" and engage with other users' content via [likes](/wiki/Like_button "Like button") or "retweets".[[119]](#cite_note-Conger_2023-119)

### Vine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Vine")]

Main article: [Vine (service)](/wiki/Vine_(service) "Vine (service)")

On October 5, 2012, Twitter acquired a video clip company called Vine that launched in January 2013.[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121) Twitter released Vine as a standalone app that allows users to create and share six-second looping video clips on January 24, 2013. Vine videos shared on Twitter are visible directly in users' Twitter feeds.[[122]](#cite_note-122) On October 27, 2016, Twitter announced that it would disable all [uploads](/wiki/File_hosting_service "File hosting service"), but that viewing and [download](/wiki/Download "Download") would continue to work.[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-Important_News_About_Vine-124) On January 20, 2017, Twitter launched an Internet archive of all Vine videos that had ever been published. The archive was officially discontinued in April 2019.[[125]](#cite_note-vine-2019-125)

### Periscope

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Periscope")]

Main article: [Periscope (service)](/wiki/Periscope_(service) "Periscope (service)")

On March 13, 2015, Twitter announced its acquisition of Periscope, an app that allowed live streaming of video.[[126]](#cite_note-126) Periscope was launched on March 26, 2015.[[127]](#cite_note-127) Due to declining usage, product realignment, and high maintenance costs the service was discontinued on March 31, 2021.[[128]](#cite_note-128) However, past Periscope videos can still be watched via Twitter and most of its core features are now incorporated into the app.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

### Crashlytics and Fabric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Crashlytics and Fabric")]

Main article: [Crashlytics](/wiki/Crashlytics "Crashlytics")

Twitter acquired Crashlytics, a crash reporting tool for developers, on January 28, 2013, for over US$100 million, its largest acquisition at the time.[[130]](#cite_note-crashlytics2-130) Twitter committed to continue supporting and expanding the service.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

In October 2014, Twitter announced Fabric, a suite of mobile developer tools built around Crashlytics.[[132]](#cite_note-132) Fabric brought together Crashlytics, Answers (mobile app analytics), Beta (mobile app distribution), Digits (mobile app identity and authentication services), MoPub, and TwitterKit (login with Twitter and Tweet display functionality) into a single, modular [SDK](/wiki/Software_development_kit "Software development kit"), allowing developers to pick and choose which features they needed while guaranteeing ease of installation and compatibility. By building Fabric on top of Crashlytics, Twitter was able to take advantage of Crashlytics' large adoption and device footprint to rapidly scale usage of MoPub and TwitterKit. Fabric reached active distribution across 1 billion mobile devices just 8 months after its launch.[[133]](#cite_note-133)

In early 2016, Twitter announced that Fabric was installed on more than 2 billion active devices and used by more than 225,000 developers. Fabric is recognized as the #1 most popular crash reporting and also the #1 mobile analytics solution among the top 200 iOS apps, beating out [Google Analytics](/wiki/Google_Analytics "Google Analytics"), [Flurry](/wiki/Flurry_(company) "Flurry (company)"), and [Mixpanel](/wiki/Mixpanel "Mixpanel").[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) In January 2017, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") acquired Fabric from Twitter and later integrated it into their [Firebase](/wiki/Firebase "Firebase") platform.[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137)

### Revue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Revue")]

Revue was a service which lets writers create email [newsletters](/wiki/Newsletter "Newsletter") and offer free or paid subscriptions to them. Revue was founded in the Netherlands in 2015 and acquired by Twitter on January 26, 2021.[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138) On December 14, 2022, Twitter announced the service would be discontinued on January 18, 2023, at which point all user data would be deleted.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

## Acquisitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Acquisitions")]

Main article: [List of mergers and acquisitions by Twitter](/wiki/List_of_mergers_and_acquisitions_by_Twitter "List of mergers and acquisitions by Twitter")

On April 11, 2010, Twitter acquired Atebits, developers of the Twitter client [Tweetie](/wiki/Tweetie "Tweetie") for the [Mac](/wiki/Macintosh "Macintosh") and [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone").[[140]](#cite_note-140)

On January 28, 2013, Twitter acquired [Crashlytics](/wiki/Crashlytics "Crashlytics") in order to build out its mobile developer products.[[130]](#cite_note-crashlytics2-130) On August 28, 2013, Twitter acquired Trendrr,[[141]](#cite_note-141) followed by the acquisition of MoPub on September 9, 2013.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

On June 4, 2014, Twitter announced that it would acquire [Namo Media](/wiki/Namo_Media "Namo Media"), a technology firm specializing in "[native advertising](/wiki/Native_advertising "Native advertising")" for mobile devices.[[143]](#cite_note-143) On June 19, 2014, Twitter announced that it had reached an undisclosed deal to buy SnappyTV, a service that helps edit and share video from television broadcasts.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) The company was helping broadcasters and rights holders to share video content both organically across social and via Twitter's Amplify program.[[146]](#cite_note-Sawers-146) In July 2014, Twitter announced that it intended to buy a young company called CardSpring for an undisclosed sum. CardSpring enabled retailers to offer online shoppers coupons that they could automatically sync to their credit cards in order to receive discounts when they shopped in physical stores.[[147]](#cite_note-147) On July 31, 2014, Twitter announced that it had acquired a small password-security startup called Mitro.[[148]](#cite_note-148) On October 29, 2014, Twitter announced a new partnership with [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"). The partnership was intended to help businesses use Twitter data to understand their customers, businesses and other trends.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

On February 11, 2015, Twitter announced that it had acquired Niche, an advertising network for social media stars, founded by [Rob Fishman](/wiki/Rob_Fishman "Rob Fishman") and Darren Lachtman.[[150]](#cite_note-150) The acquisition price was reportedly $50 million.[[151]](#cite_note-151) Twitter announced that it had acquired TellApart, a commerce ads tech firm, with $532 million stock.[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153) In June 2016, Twitter [[154]](#cite_note-154) acquired an [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") startup called Magic Pony for $150 million.[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-156)

On January 26, 2021, Twitter acquired Revue, an email newsletter service to compete with platforms like [Substack](/wiki/Substack "Substack").[[138]](#cite_note-:0-138)

In November and December 2021, Twitter acquired and immediately shut down two competitors: threader.app, a service to transform Twitter threads into [accessible](/wiki/Accessibility "Accessibility") web pages, and Quill, a messaging service.[[157]](#cite_note-157) Threader.app users were directed to instead purchase the [Twitter Blue](/wiki/Twitter_Blue "Twitter Blue") service, which at the time was available only in some countries.[[158]](#cite_note-158)

## Leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Leadership")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Jack_Dorsey_-_TechCrunch_Real-Time_Stream_Crunchup_-_2009.jpg/250px-Jack_Dorsey_-_TechCrunch_Real-Time_Stream_Crunchup_-_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jack_Dorsey_-_TechCrunch_Real-Time_Stream_Crunchup_-_2009.jpg)

[Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey"), co-founder and then CEO of Twitter, in 2009

As [chief executive officer](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer"), Dorsey saw the startup through two rounds of capital funding by the [venture capitalists](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") who backed the company.[[159]](#cite_note-bits-159) On October 16, 2008,[[160]](#cite_note-160) Williams took over the role of CEO, and Dorsey became chairman of the board.[[161]](#cite_note-161) On October 4, 2010, Williams announced that he was stepping down as CEO. [Dick Costolo](/wiki/Dick_Costolo "Dick Costolo"), formerly Twitter's [chief operating officer](/wiki/Chief_operating_officer "Chief operating officer"), became CEO. On October 4, 2010, Williams made an announcement saying that he will stay with the company and "be completely focused on product strategy".[[162]](#cite_note-162)[[163]](#cite_note-163)

According to *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, "Mr. Dorsey and Mr. Costolo forged a close relationship" when Williams was away.[[164]](#cite_note-NYT20110328-164) According to *[PC Magazine](/wiki/PC_Magazine "PC Magazine")*, Williams was "no longer involved in the day-to-day goings on at the company". He was focused on developing a new startup, and became a member of Twitter's board of directors, and promised to "help in any way [he could]."[[165]](#cite_note-165) Dorsey rejoined Twitter in March 2011, as executive chairman focusing on product development. At that time, he split his schedule with [Square](/wiki/Square_(payment_service) "Square (payment service)") (where he is CEO), whose offices are within walking distance of Twitter's in San Francisco.[[164]](#cite_note-NYT20110328-164)

In September 2011, board members and investors [Fred Wilson](/wiki/Fred_Wilson_(financier) "Fred Wilson (financier)") and Bijan Sabet resigned from Twitter's board of directors.[[166]](#cite_note-166) In October 2012, Twitter announced it had hired former Google executive Matt Derella to become their new director of business agency development.[[167]](#cite_note-167) Twitter named former Goldman Sachs executive Anthony Noto as the company's CFO in July 2014, with an "annual salary of $250,000 and one-time restricted stock options of 1.5 million shares ... valued at $61.5 million".[[168]](#cite_note-168) On June 10, 2015, Twitter announced its CEO [Dick Costolo](/wiki/Dick_Costolo "Dick Costolo") would resign on July 1, 2015.[[169]](#cite_note-169) Noto was said to be considered a potential replacement for outgoing CEO Costolo.[[170]](#cite_note-170) On October 14, 2015, former Google chief business officer [Omid Kordestani](/wiki/Omid_Kordestani "Omid Kordestani") became executive chairman, replacing Dorsey who remains CEO.[[171]](#cite_note-171) On January 26, 2016, [Leslie Berland](/wiki/Leslie_Berland "Leslie Berland"), former executive vice president of global advertising, marketing, and digital partnerships at [American Express](/wiki/American_Express "American Express"), was named chief marketing officer.[[172]](#cite_note-172) In November 2016, COO Adam Bain announced his resignation and CFO Anthony Noto took over Bain's role.[[173]](#cite_note-173)[[174]](#cite_note-174) A month later, on December 20, 2016, CTO Adam Messinger announced that he too was leaving.[[175]](#cite_note-175)[[176]](#cite_note-176)

In February 2020, it was reported that [Elliott Management Corporation](/wiki/Elliott_Management_Corporation "Elliott Management Corporation") had acquired a stake in Twitter, with [activist shareholder](/wiki/Activist_shareholder "Activist shareholder") and [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") supporter [Paul Singer](/wiki/Paul_Singer_(businessman) "Paul Singer (businessman)") expected to seek the removal of Dorsey as CEO.[[177]](#cite_note-177) Twitter agreed to appoint a new independent director and two new board members, and to perform $2 billion in share buybacks.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

On November 29, 2021, Jack Dorsey stepped down as CEO. He was replaced by CTO Parag Agrawal.[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180) On October 27, 2022, [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk") closed a deal to purchase the company and fired Agrawal, CFO Ned Segal, chief legal officer [Vijaya Gadde](/wiki/Vijaya_Gadde "Vijaya Gadde"), and general counsel Sean Edgett.[[74]](#cite_note-closed-74) Musk replaced the prior board as the sole director of Twitter and appointed himself as the CEO.[[84]](#cite_note-Euronews-84)

### Funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Funding")]

Twitter raised over US$57 million from [venture capitalist](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") growth funding, although exact figures are not publicly disclosed. Twitter's first A round of funding was for an undisclosed amount that is rumored to have been between US$1 million and US$5 million.[[191]](#cite_note-MarketingVOX-191) Its second B round of funding in 2008 was for US$22 million[[192]](#cite_note-192) and its third C round of funding in 2009 was for US$35 million from Institutional Venture Partners and [Benchmark Capital](/wiki/Benchmark_Capital "Benchmark Capital") along with an undisclosed amount from other investors including [Union Square Ventures](/wiki/Union_Square_Ventures "Union Square Ventures"), [Spark Capital](/wiki/Spark_Capital "Spark Capital"), and [Insight Venture Partners](/wiki/Insight_Venture_Partners "Insight Venture Partners").[[191]](#cite_note-MarketingVOX-191) Twitter is backed by Union Square Ventures, Digital Garage, Spark Capital, and [Bezos Expeditions](/wiki/Bezos_Expeditions "Bezos Expeditions").[[159]](#cite_note-bits-159)

The company raised US$200 million in new venture capital in December 2010, at a valuation of approximately US$3.7 billion.[[193]](#cite_note-wsjr-193) In August 2010 Twitter announced a "significant" investment led by [Digital Sky Technologies](/wiki/Digital_Sky_Technologies "Digital Sky Technologies") that, at US$800 million, was reported to be the largest [venture round](/wiki/Venture_round "Venture round") in history.[[194]](#cite_note-194) In December 2011, the [Saudi](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") prince [Alwaleed bin Talal](/wiki/Alwaleed_bin_Talal "Alwaleed bin Talal") invested US$300 million in Twitter. The company was valued at US$8.4 billion at the time.[[195]](#cite_note-195)

### Stock launch and tax issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Twitter,_Inc.&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Stock launch and tax issues")]

On September 12, 2013, Twitter announced that it had filed papers with the [U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission") (SEC) ahead of a planned stock market listing.[[196]](#cite_note-196) It revealed its [prospectus](/wiki/Prospectus_(finance) "Prospectus (finance)") in an 800-page filing.[[197]](#cite_note-197) Twitter planned to raise US$1 billion as the basis for its stock market debut.[[198]](#cite_note-198) The [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") (IPO) filing states that "200,000,000+ monthly active users" access Twitter and "500,000,000+ tweets per day" are posted.[[199]](#cite_note-Inc-199)[[200]](#cite_note-200) In an October 15, 2013, amendment to their SEC [S-1](/wiki/Form_S-1 "Form S-1") filing,[[201]](#cite_note-201) Twitter declared that they would list on the [New York Stock Exchange (NYSE)](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange"), quashing speculation that their stock would trade on the [NASDAQ](/wiki/NASDAQ "NASDAQ") exchange. This decision was widely viewed to be a reaction to the botched [initial public offering of Facebook](/wiki/Initial_public_offering_of_Facebook "Initial public offering of Facebook").[[202]](#cite_note-202) On November 6, 2013, 70 million shares[[203]](#cite_note-203) were priced at US$26 and issued by lead underwriter [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs").[[204]](#cite_note-204)

On November 7, 2013, the first day of trading on the NYSE, Twitter shares opened at $26.00 and closed at US$44.90, giving the company a valuation of around US$31 billion.[[205]](#cite_note-205) Consequently, executives and early investors marginally increased their capital, including co-founders Williams and Dorsey who received a sum of US$2.56 billion and US$1.05 billion respectively, while [Costolo](/wiki/Dick_Costolo "Dick Costolo")'s payment was US$345 million.[[206]](#cite_note-206) On February 5, 2014, Twitter published its first results as a public company, showing a net loss of $511 million in the fourth quarter of 2013.[[207]](#cite_note-207)

In November 2017, the [Paradise Papers](/wiki/Paradise_Papers "Paradise Papers"), a set of confidential [electronic documents](/wiki/Electronic_document "Electronic document") relating to [offshore investment](/wiki/Offshore_investment "Offshore investment"), revealed that Twitter is among the corporations that avoided paying taxes by using [offshore companies](/wiki/Offshore_company "Offshore company").[[208]](#cite_note-208) Later *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that Russian-American billionaire [Yuri Milner](/wiki/Yuri_Milner "Yuri Milner") had strong [Kremlin](/wiki/Moscow_Kremlin "Moscow Kremlin") backing for his investments in Facebook and Twitter.[[209]](#cite_note-209)
